content:
# 2 Thessalonians Chapter 3 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF page 205 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2TH_003_page_001.jpg

1. Finally, brothers, pray for us, that the word of the Lord may run and be glorified, just as it is also with you,

2. and that we may be delivered from perverse and evil men; for not all have the faith.

3. But faithful is the Lord, who will establish you and guard you from the evil one.

4. And we have confidence in the Lord concerning you, that what we command you, you both do and will do.

5. And may the Lord direct your hearts into the love of God and into the steadfastness of Christ.

6. Now we command you, brothers, in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, to withdraw yourselves from every brother who walks in a disorderly manner and not according to the tradition which they received from us.

7. For you yourselves know how it is necessary to imitate us, because we were not disorderly among you,

8. nor did we eat bread from anyone without paying, but in toil and hardship, working night and day, so as not to be a burden to any of you —

9. not because we do not have the right, but in order that we might give ourselves as an example to you, so that you would imitate us.

10. For even when we were with you, we used to command you this: that if anyone is not willing to work, neither let him eat.

11. For we hear that some among you are walking in a disorderly manner, not working at all but being busybodies.

12. Now such persons we command and exhort in the Lord Jesus Christ, that working with quietness they eat their own bread.

13. But you, brothers, do not grow weary in doing good.

14. And if anyone does not obey our word through this letter, take note of that person and do not associate with him, so that he may be put to shame.

15. And do not regard him as an enemy, but admonish him as a brother.

16. Now may the Lord of peace himself give you peace at all times in every way. The Lord be with you all.

17. The greeting is in my own hand — Paul — which is a sign in every letter; this is how I write.

18. The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you all.

---
## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **"May run and be glorified" (v.1):** τρέχῃ καὶ δοξάζηται — Paul personifies the word of the Lord as a runner in the arena. The imagery recalls Psalm 147:15 (LXX 147:4), where God's word "runs swiftly." The passive δοξάζηται indicates that the word is glorified not by human effort but by its own inherent power as it accomplishes its purpose. Sinaiticus reads the text consistently with the broader manuscript tradition here.

2. **"Perverse and evil" (v.2):** ἀτόπων καὶ πονηρῶν ἀνθρώπων — ἄτοπος literally means "out of place," hence "perverse, improper, unreasonable." Paul likely refers to specific opponents of the gospel mission, possibly in Corinth where 2 Thessalonians was probably written. The article τῆς πίστεως ("the faith") with the definite article suggests not merely subjective believing but the objective content of faith — the gospel message itself.

3. **"The tradition which they received from us" (v.6):** Sinaiticus reads παρελαβοσαν ("they received") rather than the variant παρελαβετε ("you received") found in some later manuscripts. This third-person reading shifts the emphasis: the tradition is an established, publicly transmitted body of apostolic teaching, not merely a private instruction to the Thessalonians. The formal equivalence rendering preserves this distinctive Sinaiticus reading.

4. **"Working night and day" (v.8):** νυκτὸς καὶ ἡμέρας — the genitive of time indicates continuous action spanning both night and day. Paul's manual labor (likely tentmaking, cf. Acts 18:3) was not incidental but a deliberate apostolic strategy. He had the "right" (ἐξουσία, v.9) to receive support but chose to forgo it as a pedagogical example.

5. **"Not working but being busybodies" (v.11):** μηδὲν ἐργαζομένους ἀλλὰ περιεργαζομένους — a wordplay in Greek between ἐργάζομαι ("to work") and περιεργάζομαι ("to be a busybody, to work around"). The paronomasia is deliberate and rhetorically powerful. These individuals may have stopped working due to eschatological excitement about the Lord's imminent return (cf. 2:1-2), redirecting their energy into meddling in others' affairs.

6. **"The greeting in my own hand" (v.17):** ὁ ἀσπασμὸς τῇ ἐμῇ χειρὶ Παύλου — Paul takes the pen from his amanuensis to write the closing personally. He calls this a "sign" (σημεῖον) authenticating every letter, which gains particular force given the concern in 2:2 about forged letters circulating under Paul's name. The autograph greeting serves as an anti-forgery measure.

7. **"With you all" (v.18):** μετὰ πάντων ὑμῶν — Sinaiticus includes πάντων ("all"), consistent with Paul's inclusive concern for the entire congregation, including those who have been walking disorderly. The grace benediction embraces even those who have been admonished. The book concludes with a subscript ΠΡΟΣ ΘΕΣΣΑΛΟΝΙΚΕΙΣ Β visible in the manuscript before Hebrews begins in column 4.

— LOGOS, AI Translator
